primary_diagnosis.name: Severe epistaxis with posterior component, unresponsive to first aid

pathophysiology_summary: Profuse nasal bleeding that has failed to arrest after 45 minutes of correctly applied direct pressure, with blood tracking down the posterior pharynx causing swallowing and vomiting, indicates severe epistaxis with a posterior component and/or a vessel not amenable to simple anterior compression. Regular aspirin use impairs platelet aggregation, worsening the bleeding. Continued swallowing of blood risks aspiration and vomiting, while ongoing blood loss risks hypovolaemic shock, reflected here by tachycardia and borderline hypotension. This requires urgent in-person intervention (topical vasoconstrictor, chemical cautery, or anterior/posterior nasal packing) that cannot be performed via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Severe epistaxis unresponsive to first aid
differential.evidence_basis: 45 minutes of correct pressure with ongoing bleeding and posterior flow.
Would be the default assumption for most nosebleeds, but failure after 45 minutes of correct pressure excludes this.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Failure of properly-applied direct pressure for this long should immediately distinguish this from a typical self-limiting nosebleed.

differential.diagnosis: Severe epistaxis with posterior component and aspiration/hypovolaemia risk
differential.evidence_basis: Vomiting swallowed blood and lightheadedness (DI-002, DI-004) confirm significant ongoing blood loss.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage, urgent in-person intervention should be clearly recommended.

differential.diagnosis: Severe epistaxis with posterior component, unresponsive to first aid
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring urgent in-person nasal packing/cautery and monitoring for blood loss.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan recommending only continued home pressure is a critical safety failure given the already-failed 45 minutes of first aid.

red_flags.description: Failure of 45 minutes of correctly applied direct pressure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates a bleeding source not amenable to simple compression, requiring professional intervention.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued blood loss and delayed definitive treatment.

red_flags.description: Posterior pharyngeal blood flow with swallowed-blood vomiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Aspiration risk and marker of significant ongoing blood loss.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Aspiration or progression to hypovolaemic shock.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia and borderline hypotension on examination (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective evidence of early volume depletion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Repeating first-aid advice instead of escalating
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might simply re-explain proper nosebleed first aid technique without recognising that correctly-applied pressure has already failed for 45 minutes, meaning the correct next step is urgent in-person intervention, not more home pressure.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct visualisation of the bleeding vessel
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent in-person assessment (ED or urgent care with ENT capability) for nasal packing/cautery; call 000 if severe dizziness, chest pain, or worsening bleeding develop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Continuing to recommend home first aid after it has already failed for 45 minutes risks ongoing significant blood loss and aspiration.
